name: subscription-detection
description: A normal Claude Code Pro/Max session runs one moderate turn, then sits alive long enough for at least one asynchronous statusline tick to arrive. The user-observable point is that a subscription (Claude.ai Pro/Max) session is detected as such: Claude Code's statusLine command POSTs a payload carrying a `rate_limits` block to the daemon (POST /api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline), and the daemon's statuslineToSnapshot() turns that into a non-nil RateLimitSnapshot with at least one Window. API-key/Bedrock/Vertex sessions get no rate_limits block and produce a nil snapshot — that absence-variant is the separate, deferred subscription-detection-apikey cell, not this one. Why this exact shape: the statusLine hook fires on the Claude Code CLI's own timer, NOT synchronously with the turn, so the recipe keeps the process alive with extra no-op turns and a generous trailing sleep (>=15s) after the last turn so at least one statusline POST lands and is recorded before teardown — a one-shot --print run would exit before any tick fired. The settings blob is intentionally empty `{}`: claude --settings MERGES with the developer's global ~/.claude/settings.json, whose statusLine.command tees the payload to http://localhost:7837/.../statusline, so the Pro/Max rate_limits POST happens with no per-scenario setup. How it differs between runs: the rate-limit UsedPercent / ResetsAt values are live account numbers and vary every run; token counts and assistant wording vary; the lifecycle state arc (ready->working->ready) and the presence of a non-nil snapshot do not. Where to look if it breaks: confirm the running daemon is on :7837 with --record (attach mode), that ~/.claude/settings.json still has the statusLine curl to /api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline, and that the session was Pro/Max (not API-key) — an API-key session legitimately yields a nil snapshot and belongs to subscription-detection-apikey.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Subscription detection asks whether the agent surfaces a billing-model signal — a subscription plan vs. an API-key/credits path. The two adapters that support it do so because the agent emits the data: Codex carries `plan_type` / `credits` in its `rate_limits` map (`extractCodexRateLimits` in core/adapters/inbound/agents/codex/parser.go), and Claude Code's statusline hook includes a `rate_limits` block for subscription users (statuslineToSnapshot in core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/statusline.go).

Aider has no such concept. It is a local CLI that talks to a provider using the user's own API key (or a local model); there is no Aider-managed subscription tier, no plan-type field, and no credits ledger in its output. The aider adapter accordingly has zero rate-limit / plan-type / credits / subscription extraction — `grep -ri 'rate_limit|plan_type|quota|credits|subscription' core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/` returns nothing.

The **Primitive exercised** (subscription / billing-model detection) maps to the `cost_tracking` key in replaydata/agents/aider/capabilities.json, which is `false` — Aider's BYO-key model has no billing/subscription state to surface. This is the sibling of `quota-burndown`.

## Two-axis derivation (#476)

Because `agent_supports: no`, `daemon_capability` is `n/a` per the matrix convention: there is no signal in the transcript and no parser code path that could ever observe one, so there is no observation question to answer.

`driver_capability` is `ready` (frozen): the cell is authored as an assessment only, so the absence of any required driver step is not a gap — the interactive driver is not exercised. The route is `frozen` because the agent lacks the feature, not because of any daemon or driver limitation.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's OK)

Everything billing-related. The feature does not exist in the agent under test — Aider's BYO-key model has no subscription state to report — so the absence of observation is correct, not a gap.
caveats: agent_supports=no, so this cell is authored as an assessment only — no recording, expected.jsonl, or transcript.jsonl.
A future Aider release that introduced a hosted/subscription mode emitting plan data would change this verdict.

sources.note: No rate-limit / plan-type / credits / subscription extraction exists in the aider adapter — nothing to parse a billing-model signal from.
cost_tracking=false — the Primitive exercised (billing-model detection) capability key is absent for aider; no subscription state exists to observe.
Contrast: Codex extracts plan_type/credits from rate_limits — the signal subscription-detection asserts, absent in Aider.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code emits subscription quota data via the statusline hook (POST /api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline). The key billing-model signal is structural: **subscription users get a `rate_limits` block in the hook payload; API-key, Bedrock, and Vertex users do not**.

The statusline handler in `core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/statusline.go` converts the hook payload to a `session.RateLimitSnapshot` via `statuslineToSnapshot()`. The function returns nil when `rate_limits` is absent (API-key path) or when both `five_hour` and `seven_day` windows are nil. This nil/non-nil distinction is the irrlicht-observable subscription signal for Claude Code — PlanType is not populated from the statusline (the hook payload carries no plan_type field).

For the subscription variant of this scenario, the recording needs a Pro/Max session. The developer's normal session qualifies. A single turn is sufficient to trigger the statusline hook and produce a non-nil snapshot.

For the API-key variant, the recording requires a session backed by an API key (not a Pro subscription), so `rate_limits` is absent from the statusline payload. This requires a dedicated test key distinct from the developer's Pro session and is deferred to a separate fixture: `subscription-detection-apikey`.

## What irrlicht does NOT see (and why that's OK)

Claude Code's statusline does not include a `plan_type` string (pro, max, team). The only plan-type signal available is the presence of the `rate_limits` block itself, which distinguishes subscription from API-key. Finer-grained plan type (pro vs max) is not surfaced via the statusline hook today. The scenario therefore validates presence/absence of RateLimitSnapshot, not the value of PlanType.
caveats: Recording the subscription variant requires the developer's normal Claude Code Pro/Max session — no special key needed.
The API-key variant (RateLimitSnapshot nil) is deferred to a separate scenario ID (subscription-detection-apikey) because it requires a different Claude Code session backed by an API key.

The statusline hook fires asynchronously on a timer in the Claude Code CLI; the recording script must keep the session alive long enough for at least one statusline tick to arrive after the turn completes. Typically one turn of moderate length is sufficient.

sources.note: statuslineToSnapshot() returns nil when rate_limits is absent or both windows are nil — the nil/non-nil distinction is the subscription signal.
Comment explicitly states: 'API-key users, Bedrock, and Vertex don't get the rate_limits block'.
Subscription variant: RateLimitSnapshot non-nil with at least one Window entry.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated as a Claude.ai Pro/Max subscription session (NOT an API key) — the subscription is what makes the statusline payload carry a rate_limits block
irrlichd is recording on :7837 with --record (this cell records in --attach mode against the developer's running daemon, because claudecode's statusLine hook POSTs to a hardcoded localhost:7837)

the developer's global ~/.claude/settings.json defines statusLine.command teeing the payload to http://localhost:7837/api/v1/hooks/claudecode/statusline (claude --settings merges with global settings, so the empty scenario settings blob does not disable this)
no other claudecode session uses the same UUID
setup: Generate a fresh lowercased session UUID (run-cell.sh does this via uuidgen | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]')
Create an empty cwd under <staging>/cwd/ (claude prompts for trust on first encounter — the driver auto-accepts)

script.text: List the first five prime numbers, one per line, and nothing else.
Reply with exactly the word: ok

verify: events.jsonl contains a transcript_new event for the UUID session and a state_transition to ready (session detected in ready)
events.jsonl shows a ready -> working -> ready arc for the first turn (user prompt starts a turn, turn completes back to ready)

the daemon ingested at least one rate-limit snapshot for this session during the run (subscription signal): the statusline receiver logged 'ingested rate-limit snapshot', i.e. a non-nil RateLimitSnapshot with at least one Window (five_hour and/or seven_day) was attached to the session — this is the presence-of-rate_limits subscription evidence

no API-key path: the run did NOT log 'received statusline tick with no rate_limits block' as the only statusline outcome (that would indicate the session was not a subscription, which belongs to subscription-detection-apikey)

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Codex embeds billing-model information directly in the JSONL transcript. Every `event_msg` with `type="token_count"` carries a `rate_limits` map containing:
- `plan_type` — non-empty string (e.g. "plus") for subscription sessions; empty or absent for API-key path.
- `credits` — a nested map present only on the credits/API-key path, with fields `has_credits`, `unlimited`, `remaining`, and `total`.

The `extractCodexRateLimits()` function in `core/adapters/inbound/agents/codex/parser.go` reads these fields and populates `tailer.RateLimitSnapshot.PlanType` and `.Credits`. This is later projected to `session.RateLimitSnapshot` at the metrics adapter boundary.

For the subscription variant: a Codex Plus session emits `plan_type="plus"` on each token_count event. `RateLimitSnapshot.PlanType` is non-empty and `Credits` is nil.
For the API-key/credits variant: `plan_type` is absent; `credits.has_credits=true` with remaining/total values. `RateLimitSnapshot.PlanType` is empty and `Credits` is non-nil.

Both variants are observable from the JSONL transcript alone — no out-of-band hook needed. This makes Codex the cleanest adapter for subscription detection testing.

Bugs #361 (split token_count events producing $0 cost) were caught in token-accounting; this scenario specifically validates that PlanType/Credits routing is correct, not just that token math works.
caveats: The subscription variant (plan_type=plus) requires a Codex session backed by an OpenAI Plus subscription.
The API-key/credits variant requires a prepaid credits account. Both variants can be recorded independently; the subscription variant is recorded here first.

Schema versioning: v1 Codex transcripts (Oct 2025) do not carry plan_type or credits. The fixture must use a v2+ session (Nov 2025 onward) to exercise the subscription detection path. The parser handles both versions but only v2+ has the billing-model fields.

sources.note: extractCodexRateLimits() reads plan_type and credits from the rate_limits map on token_count events. Schema version dispatch at lines 338-349.
RateLimitSnapshot domain type: PlanType string, Credits *CreditsSnapshot, Windows []RateLimitWindow.
Subscription variant: PlanType non-empty; API-key variant: Credits non-nil, PlanType empty.
